More Than Strata · Installation Guide

Weekly Status Report

A tool for tracking your billing, ticket responsiveness, and project progress — run on your own machine, updated each Monday.

Prepared for: Matt Wrigley
Issued by: Bill Maiden
Version: 1.0 · April 2026

Contents

  • 1. What this tool shows you
  • 2. Installing on your machine
  • 3. Adding your first project (manually)
  • 4. Bulk-adding many projects at once
  • 5. The six tabs explained
  • 6. Working with project milestones
  • 7. The Monday morning routine
  • 8. Frequently asked questions

1. What this tool shows you

The Weekly Status Report brings three streams of information into a single view: your billing, the health of your ticket queue, and the state of your active projects. Each section updates from a different source.

Billing & Utilisation

Weekly billable hours and billing amount, shown against the agreed weekly target. You enter these manually from Schedule B.

Ticket Hygiene

How the Zendesk queue is tracking week-to-week — tickets received, tickets solved, first response time, backlog, and live response-quality metrics.

Project Progress

A live register of your active projects. Each project is a sequence of milestones with target dates — the tool computes on-track / at-risk / blocked automatically.

You supply the tool with three things each Monday. Here is exactly where each comes from:
InputSourceHow to get it
Schedule B numbersSchedule B — Service Tracker (MTS)Read off hours billed and billing amount (ex GST) for the week just ended.
Dashboard PDFMatt Dashboard — in Zendesk ExploreOpen the Matt Dashboard in Zendesk Explore → click Download to save the PDF.
Tickets CSVMatthew: Open and Pending Tickets view — in Zendesk (not "My Unsolved Tickets")Open that view → click Actions (top-right) → Export as CSV.

1.1 Billing & Utilisation

You record two numbers each Monday — hours billed and billing amount (ex GST) for the week just ended. The tool compares each to the agreed weekly target (22.5 hrs / $6,210 ex GST, stepping up to 25 hrs from 12 May).

Billing tiles: hours billed, billing amount, average hours per day, and effective rate. Green / amber / red against target.

1.2 Ticket Hygiene

Four related but distinct views of your Zendesk queue. Three draw from Matt Dashboard in Zendesk Explore (the PDF you download each Monday), and a fourth draws from the Matthew: Open and Pending Tickets view (CSV export).

Week activity — from Matt Dashboard (Zendesk Explore)

How many tickets came in this week vs went out, and how responsive the queue has been on average.

Tickets received, solved, net change (received minus solved), first response time (average), and full resolution time.

Backlog at week-end — from Matt Dashboard (Zendesk Explore)

A point-in-time snapshot of where the queue sits at the close of the reporting week.

Total backlog (Open + Pending), split by status, plus tickets waiting for a reply and ultra-long tickets (open more than 90 days).

Live response performance — from the Matthew: Open and Pending Tickets view (CSV)

Monday morning you export that view as a CSV. The tool parses it and computes six response-quality measures — most usefully, a count of tickets where a reply is overdue by more than 24 hours.

Not responded to >24 hours, no agent response ever, client waiting on a reply, oldest unanswered (days), status mismatch (Open tickets where the convention suggests Pending), and tickets with no activity for more than 30 days.

1.3 Project Progress

The Project Register is where you track active projects — the major remediation, fire, SBBIS, fencing, roof, or legal matters that run over weeks or months. Each project is a sequence of milestones (engineer engaged, tender issued, EGM held, works completed, etc.). You set target dates on each milestone and tick them off as they complete. The tool rolls this up into a portfolio view.

Portfolio scorecard: counts by status, projects with no recent activity, cumulative days of slippage against original plans. Below, two tables roll up by owner (who is the current milestone waiting on) and by stage (where in the lifecycle each project sits).

Data source tags, at a glance.
  • manual — you type it in (Schedule B hours and amount)
  • pdf — read off Matt Dashboard (Zendesk Explore → Download)
  • csv — computed from the Matthew: Open and Pending Tickets view (Actions → Export as CSV)
  • tracker — pulled live from your Project Register

2. Installing on your machine

The tool is a single web page you open from your own computer. No login, no install wizard, no server. About 5 minutes start-to-finish.

1Save the zip file to your Downloads folder
Bill will send you matt-status-report.zip. Save it wherever you like — Downloads is fine.
2Extract the zip

Right-click the zip in File Explorer and choose Extract All…. Windows will ask where to extract — any of these work:

  • Desktop — easiest to find
  • Documents — tidier
  • OneDrive — if you want the files available on multiple machines (note: data itself doesn't sync — see FAQ)

You'll end up with a folder called matt-status-report.

3Double-click launch.bat

Open the extracted folder. You'll see five files, including index.html (the tool) and launch.bat (a two-line shortcut that opens it).

Double-click launch.bat — the tool opens in your default web browser.

If Windows asks "Did you mean to run this file?" — click Run anyway.

4Bookmark the page
Press Ctrl+D and save it as Weekly Status Report. Next Monday you can open it straight from your bookmarks.
That's the install done. The tool is empty on first open. The next two sections walk through populating it.

3. Adding your first project (manually)

Start with one project end-to-end so the tool's flow is familiar. Once you've added a few this way, Section 4 shows how to bulk-load the rest.

1Open the Projects tab and click + Add project
A dialog opens.

The Add project dialog. Picking a template pre-loads the milestone sequence; the preview box shows you exactly which steps the tool will create.

2Enter the Strata Plan number
Just the number (e.g. 4874). The tool displays it as SP 4874 throughout.
3Enter the Zendesk ticket number (optional)
If this project has a tracking ticket in Zendesk, paste the ticket number here. Useful to keep the tools cross-referenced. Leave blank if not applicable.
4Give the project a descriptive name
For example: Balconies Remedial Work, Lintels, Door Frames. This is what appears in the project list.
5Pick a template

Choose the template that most closely fits the project. The preview updates to show you the milestone sequence it will create. Seven templates are built in:

TemplateUse forMilestones
Remediation (standard)Waterproofing, balcony, façade, concrete, underpinning, structural17
Fire / ComplianceFire orders, AFSS, sprinkler installations, passive fire9
Defects / SBBISBuilding Bond Scheme statutory process10
FencingBoundary fencing under Dividing Fences Act (also retaining walls)6
Roof replacementRoof projects with a single GM funding cycle10
Legal — defects claimBannermans engagements, letters of demand, litigation8
Custom (blank)Add your own milestones from scratch0

You can add, remove, or rename milestones later — the template is a starting point.

6Set the first milestone's target date
This is the target date for the first milestone in the template (e.g. "Engineer engaged" for a remediation project). The tool then cascades remaining target dates automatically using typical elapsed times for each step. You can edit any of them afterwards.
7Click Create project
The project appears in the list with its status computed. From here, click the card to expand it and see the milestone list — where the real work happens (see Section 6).
Repeat for the three or four most active projects first — the ones where the next milestone is imminent. That way your dashboard is meaningful straight away.

4. Bulk-adding many projects at once

Once the manual flow is familiar, the bulk-add dialog lets you paste your existing 30-project list in a single step.

1Open the Projects tab and click Bulk add…
A dialog opens with a large text area.

Bulk add dialog. Paste one project per line; format is PLAN | NAME | TYPE.

2Prepare your project list

One project per line, with three fields separated by the pipe character (|):

  • Plan — Strata Plan number (e.g. 4874)
  • Name — descriptive project name
  • Type — template code (see Section 3 for the list)

Type is optional; if you leave it off, the project defaults to Remediation (standard).

3Paste and click Create

Each line becomes a project with its milestone sequence pre-loaded. The first milestone's target date defaults to today — you'll want to go through each project afterwards and set a realistic target.

Example paste: 
4874 | Balconies Remedial Work, Lintels, Door Frames | remediation-standard
76375 | Water Ingress Remediation Works | remediation-standard
101110 | Lift Door Compliance | fire-compliance
33333 | Boundary Fencing Dispute | fencing
105746 | Building Bond Scheme (SBBIS) | sbbis
22531 | Roof Replacement | roof

5. The six tabs explained

Tab 1 · Dashboard

The current week's view across all four streams. Each tile is tagged with its source and colour-coded against targets.

Dashboard — billing, weekly activity, backlog, live response performance, project portfolio, and the week's narrative, on one page.

Tab 2 · Projects

The register. Filter chips at the top, one card per project. Click a card to expand and see milestones.

Projects tab — the scorecard at top updates live as you edit individual projects below.

Tab 3 · Trends

Every metric as a 12-week sparkline. Useful for seeing direction, especially after a few weeks of data.

Trends — metric by metric, with the latest vs prior week delta coloured by whether the change is an improvement.

Tab 4 · Enter Week's Data

The Monday form. See Section 7.

Tab 5 · History & Export

Flat table of every saved week. Click Export all data (JSON) regularly — that's your backup. See FAQ.

Tab 6 · How to Use

In-app quick reference. Covers the same ground as this PDF, condensed.

6. Working with project milestones

The core of the tool. Every project is a sequence of milestones. Each milestone has a target date, an owner, and a completion flag. The tool computes each project's RAG status and slippage automatically.

Clicking a project card expands it. This example shows SP 87409 Fire Upgrades with three milestones complete (ticked), the current milestone highlighted in yellow (Committee approval, overdue — so the project is flagged Blocked), and future milestones below.

How the tool computes RAG

StatusMeaningWhen it's set
On trackCurrent milestone's target date is in the futureDefault
At riskCurrent milestone's target is within 7 daysComputed
Blocked / OverdueCurrent milestone's target date has passed, or you've flagged it manuallyComputed or manual override
PlanningNo target date set yet on the current milestoneBrand new project

Five actions per project

1Tick a milestone to mark it complete
The next uncompleted milestone becomes the new current one. Status recomputes instantly.
2Edit a target date to reflect a new plan
Click the date field and pick a new date. If the new date is later than the original, a red +Nd slip appears. The original date stays locked — that's how cumulative slippage is tracked across the portfolio.
3Change the owner
Pick who the milestone is waiting on (Committee, Engineer, Contractor, Owners Corporation, etc.). The dashboard rolls these up — "4 milestones waiting on Committee, 2 on Engineer" — so you can see where the portfolio is held up.
4Add a milestone
Click + Add milestone at the bottom of the list. Useful for project-specific steps the template didn't include.
5Override RAG manually
If you know a project is blocked before the target date passes (e.g. a consultant has gone unresponsive), use the Override RAG dropdown and type a short reason.
The aging rule. If you don't touch a project for 14 days, the tool flags it 14d no touch. The Monday entry form surfaces these so you can confirm them still active or archive — the aim is simply to keep the portfolio counts truthful.

7. The Monday morning routine

About 10 minutes of work once you have a rhythm.

Enter Week's Data — billing, then PDF upload with inline preview, then CSV, then the portfolio scorecard and two short narrative boxes.

1Download Matt Dashboard (PDF) from Zendesk Explore
In Zendesk, open Zendesk Explore and go to the dashboard named "Matt Dashboard". Set the time filter to Previous week (7 days ending Sunday). Click Download to save it as a PDF to your Downloads folder.
2Export the Matthew: Open and Pending Tickets view as CSV
In Zendesk, open the view called "Matthew: Open and Pending Tickets" — not "My Unsolved Tickets". Click Actions (top-right of the view), then Export as CSV. The file downloads to your Downloads folder.
3Pull Schedule B totals
Hours billed and billing amount (ex GST) for the week just ended.
4Open the tool → Enter Week's Data
Week Ending defaults to the Sunday just past. Type billing hours and amount.
5Upload the PDF
It displays inline. Read tile numbers off the preview and type them into the fields.
6Upload the CSV
The tool parses it and computes the live response metrics.
7Glance at the portfolio scorecard
If any projects are flagged Aged 14+d, switch to the Projects tab and either confirm still active or archive.
8Write the two narrative boxes
What moved this week — 3–5 bullets naming specific projects.
What's stuck — projects that are blocked or at risk, with a one-line reason.
9Preview → Save Week
Dashboard and Trends update instantly.

8. Frequently asked questions

Where is my data stored?

In your web browser's local storage, on the machine you opened the tool on. Nothing is sent anywhere. If you clear your browser data, your history is lost — so export JSON regularly from the History tab.

Can I use the tool from more than one machine?

Yes, but data doesn't sync automatically. Workflow: export JSON from machine A → save to OneDrive or email it to yourself → import on machine B. A future Azure-hosted version will handle syncing for you.

What if I open the tool in a different browser?

Each browser has separate storage. Export from the one you've been using and import in the new one.

Do I need internet?

No. The tool runs entirely in your browser. Internet is only needed to pull the Zendesk exports.

I use Edge / Chrome / Firefox / Safari — does it matter?

Any modern browser works. Edge is installed by default on Windows and is a fine choice.

What if a project doesn't need an EGM or Strata Loan?

The Remediation template marks those milestones as "optional". Tick them completed to skip past, or delete them from that project.

What if I make a mistake when saving a week?

Save again with the same Week Ending date — the entry is replaced.

Can Bill see my data?

Only if you send it. The tool is fully local. The future Azure-hosted version will give Bill and Kat read access via Microsoft 365 sign-in — but that's a separate, announced step.

How do I back up?

History tab → Export all data (JSON). Save the file to OneDrive or email it to yourself. Weekly is ideal; monthly is fine. The file is small (typically under 100 KB).

What if a template is wrong for my project?

Two options. (1) Pick the closest template, then add / remove milestones. (2) Pick Custom and build the milestone list from scratch. Any milestone can be edited or removed at any time.

What happens next?

Use it for a few weeks. We'll review together and adjust templates, thresholds, or add features based on what you find. The Azure-hosted version with automatic Zendesk integration is the next major step.
